items.q: Is language change a sign of decline?
items.correct: No — modern linguistics treats change as natural and continuous, not as decay. Every language has always been changing and will continue to.
items.misconception: Language change shows that standards are falling and English is being ruined.
items.random_wrong: Language change only happens in informal speech, not in writing.
items.why: The 'decline' view is prescriptivist folk-belief. Linguists observe that what one generation laments as 'wrong' is often what previous generations also lamented and became standard — change is constant.

items.q: What is the telegraphic stage of child language development?
items.correct: Around age 2-3, children produce short utterances containing content words but omitting function words and inflections (e.g. 'mummy gone shop').
items.misconception: It is the stage when children first start using a telephone.
items.random_wrong: It is the stage where children memorise long stories.
items.why: The name comes from telegrams — which dropped articles and prepositions to save money. Students misread the term literally rather than understanding the analogy.

items.q: What did Chomsky propose about language acquisition?
items.correct: Children possess an innate Language Acquisition Device — an inborn capacity for grammar — because exposure alone cannot account for how rapidly and uniformly children learn complex syntax.
items.misconception: Chomsky said children learn language entirely by copying adults.
items.random_wrong: Chomsky said children should be taught grammar from age two.
items.why: Chomsky's theory is precisely the opposite of behaviourist imitation. Students confuse Chomsky with Skinner — Chomsky's argument is that imitation cannot explain acquisition.

items.q: Which metaphor describes change as decay from a former golden age?
items.why: It's Crumbling castle. “Infectious disease” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Aitchison's metaphors were first delivered in which lecture series?
items.why: Correct answer: Reith Lectures. “Gifford Lectures” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Aitchison proposed how many metaphors of language change?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
